title: Unexplored 2: The Wayfarer's Legacy - a video game I recommend to fans of TTRPGs and Board Games
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=cjpnhsKzs1Q

Daniel (@dungeondive) steps back into video games to champion Unexplored 2: The Wayfarer's Legacy (Big Sugar) — an under-discussed roguelite he thinks fans of tabletop RPGs and board games will love, ranking it alongside For the King. Your quest: carry the Staff of Yonder to the hidden First Valley and destroy it, in a vast persistent world you must explore, talk through, and map to even find your destination. Characters die (permadeath), but knowledge, legacy gear and map progress carry across generations of descendants.

What makes it feel so tabletop, he argues, is everywhere: a meeple moving node-to-node across an overland map, gear that wears down to expose you to cold and wet, and — best of all — non-combat challenges run as a bag-builder, drawing success chits while dodging failures, with chosen skills (doubling as a second health pool) deepening your draw pool and a push-your-luck currency to pull more. Each chit triggers a line of emergent narrative. A huge slate of difficulty toggles lets you tune it from brutal survival to chill lore-hunting. He praises its off-the-charts sense of adventure and effortless, Runebound-like flow — a wholehearted recommendation.

----

Unexplored 2 builds its non-combat challenges as a literal bag-builder with narrative chits — a board-game mechanism done in a video game. Which tabletop ideas do you most wish more video games would borrow?
